fields.summary: 2. Visibility Infrastructure

status.description: This work item is being actively worked on at the moment by the assignee.
status.name: Advancing

content.text: We are requesting a diagram or document that maps DMSi’s server environment. This does not need to be a detailed architecture document. We need a practical, operational-level view of the infrastructure.
Server names and hostnames across the production and non-production environments
Applications and services running on each server (e.g., Progress OpenEdge, Elastic, CheckMK, NGINX, PagerDuty integrations)
Network connectivity and dependencies between servers (what talks to what)
Identification of production vs. development/staging environments
Acceptance Criteria: The Dynamo team has and understands the Architecture, Infrastructure & Network Topology landscape

This map is foundational to every workstream in our engagement. Without a clear picture of the environment, we cannot reliably plan configuration assessments, monitoring improvements, or pipeline automation work. The topology map will directly inform our interview questions, our configuration review priorities, and our recommendations for tooling and observability improvements. Delays on this request will cascade into delays across our early milestones.

fields.summary: Architecture, Infrastructure & Network Topology Map
Receive Network Diagrams (tierpoint)

content.text: generated NGinX that lives on the progressDB hosts
DMSi: Who is responsible for NGinX (Rex?)

fields.summary: Identify different NGinX deployments & Doc way forward

content.text: We had a very informative meeting with Rex today explaining the nginx deployment process and where are the configs are stored.
There are 2 main Nginx deployment types that DMSi has:

This nginx run on a pair of load balancers and does all of the top level routing. This service used to use an Nginx product with a frontend and allowed administration via web UI & API. This older product no longer exists but the 2.7Mb Nginx config that it generated still exists and is used on the global nginx LB’s. There are actually 2 sets of LB’s there is an additional hot set to the load balanced pair for failover. The configs are synced from the live pair to the hot pair.

A new global nginx config process is actively being worked on by Rex - it is not in Github. This new process uses multiple smaller .conf files that are generated by a script. Nginx pulls in all confs at runtime and acts as one.
This is the more developed nginx deployment. This nginx deployment runs on many of the Progress DB servers and does the downstream reverse-proxying.
There is a main configuration file and many smaller configs that are populated by a script at runtime to insert all the client configurations - IP’s, Certs, etc..
There is an ansible playbook that loads all of the confs on new hosts. It can also be directed at existing hosts if their nginx needs updated.
Here is a list of all files which reference NGinX in the DMSi engineering repository.
The team clarified the setup and automation process for local NGINX configuration files, focusing on a scripted approach that pulls customer-specific data and deploys updated configs efficiently.
Local NGINX Config Automation Explained by Rex Dieter
is used to create and refresh the instance created for a client.
config snipits are put in the customer folder on a sys mount and this sys mount is used all over the place.

The script parses customer XML config files from a shared network folder to extract org IDs, environment numbers, and local Tomcat ports.  It uses these values to fill in templated NGINX config files that proxy traffic from a global endpoint to local PaaS instances.
every environment has a proxy and CNAME
After generating the config, the script moves it into place and tests/reloads NGINX to apply changes immediately.
Rex emphasized this script runs during initial setup and upgrades to keep configs fresh and aligned with template changes.
Centralized Ansible Playbook for Configuration Deployment
Rex shared an Ansible playbook that installs NGINX, applies security settings, pushes config templates, and reloads services across hosts.
The playbook ensures configurations propagate globally and handles startup ordering issues with network mount dependencies via Systemd tweaks.
It supports both reverse proxying for inbound traffic and forward proxying for secure outbound calls, improving diagnostics over Progress’s native HTTP handling.
Rex noted this playbook can be rerun anytime configs need updates or new hosts are added, enabling consistent environment setup.
Current Use of Network Share for Config Storage
Config snippets are stored on a CIFS-mounted network share within customer folders, with symbolic links on local hosts.
Rex acknowledged this introduces potential startup failures when mounts are unavailable, requiring workarounds in service start ordering.
He expressed preference to move configs fully to local hosts to reduce dependencies and improve reliability.
Matthew Czaplewski highlighted the widespread use of network shares elsewhere in the org, suggesting a broader review of storage strategies.
Challenges and Improvement Opportunities in Configuration Management
Rex and Matthew discussed the potential to replace network share dependencies with GitHub or other systems for storing configs.
Rex noted that while some larger files might not fit in GitHub, many config files could benefit from version control and simpler deployment.
This shift could streamline pipeline automation by leveraging the existing Ansible playbook infrastructure.
Rex confirmed that Daniel Carnes is currently the sole admin with write permissions to this repo, restricting broader team edits.
Ongoing Work to Restore Group Access
Rex is working with a contact on the DMSI side responsible for GitHub access management to resolve this.
Proxying HTTPS Calls Through NGINX for Better Diagnostics
Progress’s native HTTP handling is weak on SSL and error messaging, causing support overhead.
Using NGINX as a forward proxy for outgoing HTTPS calls improves logging and debugging capabilities.
This setup also centralizes SSL handling, simplifying client code and improving reliability.
Because configs reside on network mounts, NGINX must start only after those mounts are available to avoid failures.
The team uses custom Systemd service files to delay NGINX startup until network shares are mounted.
Workaround would be unnecessary if configs were stored locally, simplifying startup and reducing errors.
Config files use templating with includes to share common proxy settings across customers.
Rex emphasized the configs are straightforward but must be generated per environment due to unique org and port data.
The templating approach allows easy updates to shared settings while keeping customer-specific details dynamic.  Rex invited suggestions from the team to improve or simplify these templates.
The team aligned on immediate follow-ups to improve access and start exploring configuration management improvements.
Exploration of Alternative Config Storage Strategies

Matthew Czaplewski flagged potential for moving away from network shares toward Git-based storage for configs.  Rex agreed this would reduce complexity but acknowledged the scale and impact on existing processes.
The team sees the Ansible playbook as a strong foundation to build a pipeline that integrates with GitHub-hosted configs.
Further investigation and planning will be needed to scope and implement this shift safely.
Rex confirmed willingness to provide ongoing help around the NGINX setup and automation scripts.
He also committed to updating the team on progress restoring GitHub group access.
The meeting closed with mutual thanks and encouragement to keep collaboration strong.
These positive interactions support smoother future technical coordination.
Coordinate with DMSI side personnel to restore full GitHub group access for the team
Add necessary team members to GitHub Dynamo Test repo with proper write access post-meeting
Show and explain ansible playbook used for nginx installation and config deployment to the team
Continue supporting discussions around moving nginx customer config files from network share to local storage to improve startup reliability

content.text: Recommendation on approach to managing configs in Github

fields.summary: Investigate the use of Teraform from Github actions (or other options)

content.text: After digging in to this it is pretty clear, Terraform is the industry standard for maintaining PagerDuty infra and configs. PagerDuty themselves back this method by maintaining their own first-party
The only other real contenders for an effective alternative I have seen has been Pulumni but even this seems to have far less adoption due to simplicity and readability of Terraform.
Unfortunately a bash/powershell implementation just doesn’t scale and I believe we are already seeing symptoms of that.

work_items.summary: CheckMK Cleanup
work_items.description: Owner : Faltys  DMSi SME:  Tom, Rex, Dustin--? Capability : Visibility Infrastructure Outcome : Evaluate how database calls are made to determine if we reduce CheckMK script overhead and alert noise, stabilizing the current monitoring baseline while the Dynatrace decision is pending. Details : Continue DB script reduction and consolidation work Faltys has been leading Identify scripts that generate the most alert noise vs. actual value Preserve the subset of scripts that would transfer to Dynatrace (database health queries) Coordinate with Dustin and Tom on any manually maintained CheckMK configs not previously documented Pearl and Shell scripts collection and optimization Exploration of testing env Question: Are DB calls closed after completion? Exit Criteria: Recommendation proposal for CheckMK optimizations Risks: Undocumented CheckMK scripts and configs from Dustin/Tom surfaced in planning meeting; scope may be larger than expected Over-aggressive cleanup could mask real issues during transition Work may feel low-visibility relative to other sprint areas Dependencies: Dynatrace decision (determines which scripts are temporary vs. permanent)

Details: Exit Criteria: [ ] Risks: Executive approval still required for full 360 phase-out; integration may run parallel longer than planned Dustin and Tom were previously unaware of parallel workstreams; communication gaps may resurface Security access constraints with TierPoint may limit auditing capabilities Andy is setting up a Teams channel for collaboration DONE: Andy is setting up a Teams channel for collaboration We marked   as “Won’t Do” because:  Terraform Documentation:  The standalone Terraform documentation ticket was deemed redundant; Terraform usage will instead be documented within the specific tickets where it is applied (starting with PagerDuty, WSA-111).

work_items.summary: Elastic config documented and in GitHub
work_items.description: Owner:  Matt Czaplewski To complete our Stage Zero assessment of the visibility infrastructure, we need to understand the full scope of how monitoring data is collected, processed, and stored across the environment. These artifacts are essential for identifying coverage gaps, evaluating alert quality, and developing recommendations to rationalize and improve. Exit Criteria: Source systems available integrated with Elastic are identified   and prioritized Current Elastic config is documented and saved in GitHub  Source systems not yet integrated with Elastic are identified  and prioritized Current source integrations are documented and saved in GitHub Create detailed docs for existing Elastic usage patterns Tribal knowledge captured Understanding of who changes what, how often, and why Requirements:  Read-only access to view configs on both host and clients. Blockers:   Matt blocked on GitHub access (escalated).  Elastic is limited to one host. Server/host inventory  – List of servers, CPUs, box sizes, etc. Without this, he can’t do a solid cost estimate. Cost estimation  – Build hypothetical cost tiers by host count, since many tools need host-level data for pricing. Apples-to-apples cost comparison  – Compare alternatives fairly, but he doesn’t have DMSi’s current monitoring costs yet (Cameron might help). Tool rationalization  – Document what tools they have (CheckMK, Elastic, etc.) to get a full picture. In the Feb 20 meeting, he says he’s already done “stuff around their CheckMK and their [Elastic]” and put notes in the ticket. Comparison document  – Create a breakdown for moving to Datadog (or similar), even if it’s mostly hypothetical for now. Collaboration with Carnes  – He’s sharing his work with Carnes, who can run with the tool rationalization from an infrastructure perspective.

work_items.summary: CheckMK config documented and in GitHub-Matthew
work_items.description: Owner:  Matthew Faltys |  DMSi SME:  Rex (CC Andy Myers) To complete our Stage Zero assessment of the visibility infrastructure, we need to understand the full scope of how monitoring data is collected, processed, and stored across the environment. These artifacts are essential for identifying coverage gaps, evaluating alert quality, and developing recommendations to rationalize and improve. Exit Criteria: Source systems available integrated with CheckMK are identified and prioritized Current CheckMK config is documented and saved in GitHub Source systems not yet integrated with CheckMK are identified and prioritized Current source integrations are documented and saved in GitHub Create detailed docs for existing CheckMK usage patterns Tribal knowledge captured Understanding of who changes what, how often, and why Document and review need to upgrade to 2.4 Blockers:  Host access required (formal request via Cameron). Partial scripts from Andy (one Progress DB); not versioned.

work_items.summary: Read-Only Access to Servers
work_items.description: We are requesting read-only access to the server environment so the Dynamo team can independently review installed software, configurations, and service versions without requiring DMSi team members to manually retrieve files on our behalf. What We Need Read-only SSH or RDP access to production and non-production hosts Ability to view file systems, configuration directories, and installed package versions Access scoped to observation only — no write, administrative, or modification privileges

work_items.summary: Architecture, Infrastructure & Network Topology Map
work_items.description: We are requesting a diagram or document that maps DMSi’s server environment. This does not need to be a detailed architecture document. We need a practical, operational-level view of the infrastructure. What We Need Server names and hostnames across the production and non-production environments Applications and services running on each server (e.g., Progress OpenEdge, Elastic, CheckMK, NGINX, PagerDuty integrations) Network connectivity and dependencies between servers (what talks to what) Identification of production vs. development/staging environments Acceptance Criteria: The Dynamo team has and understands the Architecture, Infrastructure & Network Topology landscape

work_items.summary: Document the retrospective format for ongoing use after Dynamo engagement
This separates teams that stay disciplined from teams that regress
Key question: "Where did work get stuck and why?" rather than "Did we finish everything?"
Introduce biweekly or monthly retrospectives focused on flow
Document any persistent bypasses and determine root cause
Validate that competing requests flow through triage, not around it
Measure side-door ratio trend: is invisible work declining?
Observe patterns over 2-3 weeks: when priorities change in the system, does the team's actual work change?
Metrics feed staffing conversations with numbers behind them, not qualitative assertions
Ensure Brent has access to capacity ratio view without attending operational ceremonies
Connect flow metrics to the Engineering Scorecard (Workstream D alignment)
These baselines feed the capacity model for staffing conversations
Document as reference ranges, not promises
Identify clusters: customer environment builds average X hours, infrastructure tickets average Y days
Pull cycle time data by category after several months of categorized work has flowed through the system
Ensure metrics are accessible without requiring manual compilation
Build views that show planned vs. reactive ratio by week, by engineer, by category
Configure cycle time, throughput, aging, and queue depth reporting in the selected tool
If shadow systems exist, determine why (friction, missing features, habit) and address root cause
Check for shadow systems: spreadsheets, personal lists, or email threads that contain work information not on the board
Informal validation: Bryan and Andy confirm the board reflects reality
If staleness persists for specific engineers, investigate whether the issue is habit, tool friction, or workload
Establish lightweight correction process: stale items flagged at triage, owner asked to update or explain
Define what "stale" means (>5 business days without update is the starting threshold)
Track freshness trend over time
Surface stale items at each triage ceremony
Implement automated or manual board freshness tracking: % of items with status update in last 5 business days
If conditions aren't met, stay at twice weekly. Weekly is the target, not a deadline
Signals the team is ready: queue inflow predictable, mid-session expedites rare, team pulling from queue consistently, triage sessions finishing under 30 minutes
Evaluate whether twice-weekly can move to weekly
Configure in the tool as board swimlanes, labels, or custom field depending on tool selected in WM-OC-03
Gives stakeholders a clear answer to "where does my request stand?" without status meetings
Introduce three commitment tiers: committed this cycle (small set, high confidence), targeted next (planned but could shift), backlog (acknowledged, not scheduled)
With several weeks of data, engineering leadership now has a real capacity ratio view
This replaces manual break-fix tagging from WM-OC-05.2 and improves data quality
PagerDuty still owns the incident lifecycle -- the integration creates work items when incidents resolve to capture follow-up work and time spent
Connect PagerDuty incidents to the work management system
By this point the team has muscle memory for intake, triage, and status updates, so extending is habit extension, not behavior change
Extend beyond customer environment configuration to include: infrastructure/platform maintenance, tooling improvements, escalation follow-up, internal requests, improvement/automation projects
Do NOT measure cycle time or throughput yet. Data won't be meaningful until at least one full cycle

Metrics: % of actual work captured (target: 80%+ within 60 days), average time from submission to triage, WIP limit adherence, board freshness, side-door capture rate, planned vs. reactive ratio (baseline only)
Measure adoption indicators, NOT performance indicators during this phase
